You're right, Mary. Half a cup is not enough to get started. Well, it's a big cup. Just clarify. It's a trough. It's half a trough of coffee. Andre has so much to contribute today. His call, though, that I'll just periodically go make myself some coffee and do a few other things while he is entertaining everyone. I think we should start on the Google Ads question. Alright, we'll do that. Alright, so welcome everybody to the Momentum Builder Workshop Lean Edition Q&A call. We don't have a time in mind for this, so we'll just go really as long as we have questions. I don't know how long that's going to be. So a few people sent questions in by email. I'm going in some people who can't attend for a variety of reasons, so I'm going to read the first question. This has been asked by other people, and I don't know if it's been asked in the comments. I know I've answered this question already, but I'll answer it again and Andre can jump in as well. So here's the question. How could I adjust the whole Momentum Builder Workshop system to Google Ads? I learned a lot about Google Ads in the traffic engine course, but it would be great if you could give further tips for sending traffic to the landing page via Google Ads. I would particularly want to know if I can use the same landing page for both Facebook Ads and Google Ads. Okay, so the answer is like everything else, it depends on the context. So when we think about Facebook, the primary distinction between Facebook traffic and Google traffic is that it's intent. So in Google, the intent is active. You're searching for something. In that moment, you want an answer to something. On Facebook, there is passive potential interest. We hope that be through a combination of targeting and other factors in our hook and other things mixed together that we capture somebody's attention. It's just the right time for them. Those are two wildly different scenarios. I don't know that they could be more different. So the initial answer is no, you can't just build something for Facebook, start to finish, and then just assume that it's going to work on Google Ads. However, this is where the nuance comes in with it depends. It certainly depends on what your offer is and how you're presenting your offer. But the way to think about Google Ads is how specific is the search? Because what is your offer and is it time-bound? Is there a need? It's something Perry Marshall used to call it, does the prospect have a bleeding neck? Does the prospect need the solution right now? So if the search intent suggests that they have this need for something right now and then you take them through this journey over seven or eight days, you're just going to piss somebody off. The example I think I used in the traffic engine, I know I've used it in other places, which is if somebody has a leaky roof and they search for roofing contractors near me, they send them to a funnel where they can download the guide to get, you know, how to save 10% off their next roof repair. That's not what they want. They want the phone number to call to get their roof fixed. So depending on your offer and depending on the intent expressed in search. So if it's a very specific search that shows clear intent that they need a solution right now, we don't want to take them on a seven plus day journey. So if the intent is less specific, so if they're more just starting to learn about something, so we can use an example, we'll use autoresponder madness. If somebody were to search for something very specific, search for autoresponder madness, for example, or search for learn email marketing today, making things up, these aren't great examples, but there would be really in all of these scenarios, I think we would take them through a very similar funnel. So I chose a bad example, which is always fun for my first, my first answer to a question. So let's let's go back to the beginning with autoresponder madness and we see if I can make this a better answer to the question. If somebody searches for learn email marketing, there's no immediate buying intent. There's no bleeding neck in that there's nothing in that search term that suggests they need a solution right now. They're early in the research phase. So because of that, it would be very appropriate to send to have a certain ad that leads directly to the landing page that you've constructed as part of the Momentum Builder workshop. But if your product or service is not like that, it's something that people might want to buy immediately. And you're bidding on search terms that suggest they want that thing right now, then you don't want to create a lot of friction in between. You would in that, in that case, I would probably send somebody right to your sales page. Like if someone is clearly showing buying intent, send them to a sales page and let them buy. If there if there's no clear intent, if it's research based terms or if it's general terms or top of the funnel type, they're just starting to learn about something, then then it would make more sense. So it depends. It's not a great answer, but it is. It's the accurate answer. And there was a follow up on this question to and then I'm going to hand it off to you. So here's the question. So what do I need to be careful about on my Google search ad regarding the theme, for example, should I be giving clues about the theme and the search ad in general probably know, right? So a successful search ad matches the intent of the person searching and makes it clear that you're the right answer for that for whatever they're searching for. That's the job of a search ad. So we're not introducing a theme at that point. We're not. The only theme that we're implying is that we're a useful answer to the search that they've just or the question they just asked. I tend to think about searches, somebody asking a question, how do I, where do I, what's the best. Even if they don't include that in the search term, it's often implied. Like someone types in email marketing. What they probably mean is how do I learn about email marketing? There's an implied question. Or what do I need to know about email marketing? Where do I start with email marketing? They don't put all of that stuff in the search term. They just start very broadly. So you don't, when you with Google ads, you don't need to worry about setting up the theme that's to come. The reason that's so important and now that that would be present in everything else that follows. But the reason that's so important with Facebook is in Facebook, nobody's looking for you. That's the critical distinction that in search, somebody is looking, they've raised their hand and they say, I want to know more about this right now in this moment. That intent is wildly different than someone scrolling through their Facebook news feed and think, oh, that looks interesting. Let me check it out. That's why we need 800, 500, 800, a thousand words in a Facebook ad to really make it clear what it's all about and why it's interesting to the person who's reading it. That's not true with Google as much because that person has in that moment already said, I'm interested, I'm looking, show me something. Anything to contribute to that, Andre? That can make a lot more sense than I just said. Go back to my previous comments earlier about not having enough coffee yet. I need to kind of get my moment of getting for the question. Yeah, I like what you said. I think it's one of the nuances or the things to wrap your head around with Google ads is that it's a challenge. It's near enough impossible in many cases to understand what the intent is behind us. Somebody, as you mentioned, doing a search for, what did you say, email marketing course? That's a good example though, right? Learn email marketing versus email marketing course. Those are very different search terms. We might think at first, blush, those are the same thing. Like, oh, someone wants to learn email marketing, they want a course, not necessarily. Somebody says, learn email marketing. You might be just looking for what can they find out for free as quickly as possible. When they add that word course, that's changed the dynamic a lot. They've become much more valuable to someone who has an email marketing course just by adding that modifier because now we know a little bit more about their search intent. Alright, so I didn't interrupt there. Because that's the case, and again, there's still a bunch of guessing involved, unless somebody is doing a search for, orders, one of madness, or some brand name. But even then, it's not an open and closed case. But you only get charged if they click on the link that goes to your website. Is that correct? So Facebook is a CPM environment, cost per thousand impressions. It can be a cost per click, but very rarely. So Facebook is CPM in general, search is cost per click. So, but it's still, it's not that cut and dry that we, what we're seeing, someone is seeing your ad when they search, they're seeing, you know, the few lines that you're presenting and essentially answering their question and they're trying to decide if you're the best answer, or they might look at three or four, you know, open them in different tabs. Facebook is different in that you're, they're very likely going to see the image in the first two lines of the ad. And the job of the image in general is to stop the scroll. So if you think about the user experience for Facebook is they're scrolling down through and the image looks interesting and just interesting enough that it slows them down. And they read the first two lines of the hook. And it, so it's a very different, like that setup, that context is just such a different entry point into everything that we've created with the reason and just so everyone understands the reason we chose Facebook for the momentum builder workshop is because it's infinitely harder to take someone who's not looking for what you have to offer at all and capture their attention and then bring them through this journey that it is when someone says, Hey, I need what you have. Show it to me. That's a that's a very if we had started with Google, we would have, we would. But because we've started with Facebook, you likely have all the ingredients that you'll need to make a version that works with Google. So yes in the traffic engine I mentioned that a high quality Facebook ad is share worthy it's written in a way where it's independently valuable. So yes, if you if you followed our suggestions in week two of the momentum builder workshop and you created a Facebook ad that way by definition it will be share worthy it will be interesting you will have created something that people within your audience will want to do. So it's not to share to others that makes sense if nobody shares that it doesn't mean you did something wrong. It's part of a larger system if it's producing customers at the end at some economically viable rate than it's successful it's part of a larger system so don't don't get hung up on the the, you know this sort of local optima, right, we don't care about local optima. We care about is the system optimum. So that's that's a sophisticated way or a complicated way of saying don't worry about all the details of the parts. Make sure that those parts are contributing and doing what they need to do for the whole. So a byproduct of that an emergent effect of a high quality Facebook ad often is it gets shared a lot. If it doesn't, don't take that to mean you did something wrong and certainly don't make that a goal. You know, it's Steven Covey said this, you know, we borrow his idea begin with the end in mind. Yeah, it's pretty nuanced. I would say in the beginning, you don't need, you know, you need to create these assets. That's, that's the point of the workshop. So if you've created all the assets beginning to end. Okay, that's, there's an option there to, to perhaps, you know, share these with the professional and get them chiseled and made better. But you don't necessarily have to do that. You could put that laugh and try and get some some data back, you know, see what's working. Because at the end of the day, when you hire somebody, they cost money. And if you can give them something that's working, that's going to cost you a lot less money, and it'll help them create something even better. But if you just give them a blank slate, and they ask you, so has this thing, this thing been tested and you say, No, that's not really helpful. I mean, they're still going to charge you. And you're still going to pay the money. But you don't have any idea of if, if the work that they've done is an improvement or not. So, yeah, I think just do the work, create the assets. And then I'll even go as far as saying, put the damn thing laugh, you know, it doesn't cost a lot of money, just cost $10 a day. That's, that's, that's, that's what it can cost. Run that for for a few days for a week for two weeks, and tweak a few things. You don't have to have world class writing chops to be able to make that work to get people to opt in. Even if, even if sales on happening, but but opt ins are that is all data that you can then offload on on somebody else who's a professional, if you wanted to bring somebody in. And I want to breach the professional slash copywriting thing just yet. I'll, I'll wait to see what you're going to say. Thanks. This is a hard question. I think so I can tell you from my experience. So this was probably, this was probably eight years of the 21 that I ran an agency. I've, I saw somewhere in the neighborhood of a dozen Facebook ads that that I call you know, I think in the traffic engine, I mentioned my column unicorn ads that they performed so power they outperformed any expectation, any, any common sense, realistic expectation of anything and when measured in terms of sales, like just, if you're thinking about an ad that was that would the purpose of the ad was to sell something eventually, whether it was over, you know, four part video series or through a landing, it didn't matter. But there were there were about a dozen that rose to this level where they the performance was if someone had told me the performance, I would have, I would have said it was not believable. I mentioned this because the thing every one of them had in common is they were not written by professionals. They were each written by the person who either own the business or was somehow directly responsible for the business. And my conclusion from that was that they're particularly in Facebook. Facebook is a social environment where where most of the content that we consume on Facebook that's not advertising is written by another human being who's not a professional writer. And when we inject into that, you know, quote unquote, copywriting, it sticks out immediately like an ad, it feels like an ad and we know and we're finally tuned as a species to know when we're when when it's something is an ad versus not so this is all a very circuitous way to come back to this point that what to you feels like unpolished writing and maybe not not great writing is most likely in a an environment like Facebook, incredibly authentic, you probably sound very real and authenticity in my observation is is something that can't be necessarily engineered I've never I haven't ever seen anybody engineer authenticity. Well, like you have to be authentic. And it's an emergent property of something else. It's not really something you can create. And when you try to create it looks like you try to create it. So, that's my way of saying, give yourself a break. Follow what Andre said first put your stuff out in the world at the best that you can create it and see what happens, because my my my suspicion is that's going to outperform anything any professional ever creates for you because you know your audience better you know your expertise better, but all of that you have so many advantages. Now, the one caveat here is, I do think there's value in finding an editor, not a writer, it's editing is so much different than writing, but to find an editor and that could be somebody in this in this workshop group you can have a collaborate you can edit each other's work. And editing doesn't have to be sophisticated it could just be somebody saying to you I don't I'm not really sure what you meant here. Could you help me understand that better, or, you know, this thing that you said here I felt like you already said this earlier, like just, it's just somebody to reflect back to you. What that like how they're feeling what they're what they're taking from your writing, which then allows you to make oftentimes allows you to make very dramatic improvements and cleaning it up in the editing process. I would be far less inclined to try to go find somebody to write and I've, I've worked with so many clients who've hired writers. I've worked with writers in the past. It is not only is it very difficult to find a good writer. It is exceptionally difficult to find a good writer who can infer your voice, and who can write in a way that makes that that's that feels like what you would write that is so difficult. So, that's a long way of saying that the way Andre described it I would, I would absolutely concur with that just do do what you can and see what happens. And then do do what you can see what happens, try to find someone to work with you to edit to clean things up, especially structurally like things flowing, do that. And then, if on the other side of that you're not getting the results that you want. Then maybe, maybe look around to try to find someone to help with the writing but that's, that's hard. I'm not that doesn't seem that I know that's not very reassuring but that that's not that's my honest answer of how I would think about it. You mentioned something that's that's a really good point and I hadn't considered that is getting an editor is is fairly easy to do and it's relatively inexpensive. You know, copywriters are not are not inexpensive. So, one of the tools that I use is the paid version of Grammarly and and and the bottom right in corner. So when you've when you've pasted in your, your email, your 1000 words or your 5000 words, whatever it is. And it's, you know, you've you've run through it and you've done all your own corrections. There's a little button at the bottom that says get expert writing help. I've never used that feature, but I've just clicked on it now and this might be available in the free version. I have no idea. But I've just clicked on it and it says three days cost $9.20 for 1000 words. So it's 0.099 cents per word. If you want to, if you want to done in three days. So that's expert help you can do very easily, relatively inexpensively with just a click using Grammarly. And then that would be that would be an extra data point. Alright, Pranam has quite a few I don't know if I'm pronouncing that correctly, there's quite a few questions here so we should probably jump on one of his. Let's just start with the first one. So I'll, I'll read the question for you, Andre, you can answer. I can't wait to see where you go with this. While you're doing that I'll change my my thoughts five times. Alright, so here's the question. This could be perhaps out of scope for this discussion but it's closely related. So I hope you entertain this one for businesses that haven't built a brand yet. In other words, don't have a huge amount of trust yet. Is it always necessary to have a tripwire offer? How else can we build a funnel? I don't remember buying any tripwire before I signed up for Andre's Sphere of Influence. I personally feel uncomfortable with low cost tripwires but I'm not sure what the alternative is. Andre Chaperon on the tripwire. Go. I can't wait to hear this. I'm so excited about this. Fine, laugh. I think I'm going to answer it by saying have you ever seen any version of a tripwire on TLB or any of the stuff that we've done? You can go to other websites and you certainly see tripwires. So I think you can deduce from that is tripwires work for some people and other people they choose not to do them. You certainly don't have to have a tripwire. Tripwire is part of a tactic to generate a low cost customer. A $7 customer and a $10 customer. As I think we all know, a customer that has purchased a tripwire is not the same customer that's going to purchase something that let's say costs $500 or $1000. That's an assumption that many marketers make because they play the numbers game and that's not the game that we play. We're in a different business. We're in this happy customer generating business. For that to happen, everything upstream needs to change and it's not the same thing. The tripwire at the top of the funnel, at the wide end, that's a completely different strategy that's going to generate a completely different type of customer or slash lead. The things that you've got to do is just going to be different. It's a choice you make. You can do stuff similar to what we do, to what Seth Godin does. There's many people that do versions of what we do or just keep pen attention to the Derek response marketers and you'll end up doing things that they do. Again, I'm not going to say what they're doing is wrong because what they're doing is they're doing something because it's working for them and that's the sort of business they want to be in. I think that's my initial answer. I think to answer this question, we also have to have some context. Everyone who knows me at this point knows I can't function without orienting us to where we are in a conversation. First of all, what's a tripwire? A tripwire is just a low cost front end offer, usually somewhere under $20. A typical funnel that uses a tripwire will have a low cost offer like free plus shipping. You get a book for free but you pay $10.95 for shipping. Then it has a bump offer. Once you've put in your info, you're like, yep, I want to do this as a checkbox that probably brings the cost up to $29 or so. Maybe you get the audio version and the PDF mind map with it. Now, your free thing that you attracted people with, just pay shipping and get this book for free, your free offer now that's the $12.95 to cover the shipping and handling. Now, you've bumped it up to $29 and then they buy it and then they get upsell number one, which is, hey, if you like this book, you're going to get... You can do this other thing, which is $49 and then often there's upsell number two, which is more in the $250 range. Then all of this is to set up eventually a core offer that's the real thing that you're selling. In the idea, there are a couple of things to understand about why people do tripwires. One, it's a way to offset the cost of paying for traffic. You're selling something because you're costing you money to get the person to buy and you want to offset the cost of that. That's one of the primary reasons. The secondary reason I think is a... It's a bait and switch idea that... When I say bait and switch, if you really understand how the world works, then you see the flawed logic. If you don't, if you just accept it at face value, it's not as easy to see the flawed logic. So here's the flawed logic. You'll often hear people who advocate for... In the term tripwire was coined by Ryan Dice, a digital marketer. It's just a low-cost front-end offer. He just gave it a good name, which is brilliant marketing and became associated with it. Everyone thinks that Ryan Dice invented the tripwire. He just gave it a name. So the logic behind it is when somebody has purchased, when you're creating a list of customers, that person is 10 times more likely to buy later. And within the direct response world, there's a bifurcation of email lists. You might have an email list of leads or you might have an email list of buyers. And if you have an email list of buyers, that's thought to be far more valuable. So if you're in the business of promoting other people's stuff and someone says, hey, I'd like to mail, how much would it cost me to mail to your list? Well, then you say, if it's a list of leads, you're going to get a lot less money than if you have a list of buyers. So because of that, incentives drive behavior. So if you the lowest threshold to get a list of buyers is to sell something inexpensive. So but here's the flawed logic. The assumption is the list of low cost buyers is 10 times more valuable than a list of just leads. But that's a list of crappy leads. So it's 10 times more valuable than basically zero. That's where the logic is flawed. And it's also flawed because people who buy $10 things tend to want to buy $10 things, especially on Facebook, when the algorithm is optimizing on your behalf. When you're selling a free plus shipping offer, you tend to find lots of people who want to buy low cost things with really outsized value propositions. You don't necessarily find $10,000 course buyers because you sold them a $9.95 book. I've worked with a lot of clients who've used free plus shipping book funnels. I've talked to an unbelievable number of people who've done this and uniformly it does not produce the results. And people like Brooke Castillo from the Life Coach School has talked about this publicly when she talked about switching over to a sphere of influence inspired front end system and how much better that was for her than a book funnel because the book funnel that was selling $10 ebooks got $10 customers. And her offer is $297 a month. There's a huge jump between a $10. It's not the same customer. So this idea that somehow there's this context independent thing we call a customer. And if you can just make one of those, then that person who just became a customer now is going to buy your $1,000 offer. That's bullshit. That's not how the world works. And we all know that intuitively. So when you know that, when you know how the world really works, it's much easier to answer this question. So yes, if you are doing high volume traffic where you need to monetize that traffic to pay for it, I think there's a case to be made that a low cost offer on the front end makes sense. Just be aware that you're still effectively building yourself a list of leads that is marginally more likely to buy an offer down the road. You just didn't have to pay for them. And if you compare that to paying more for your leads upfront, it costs more to get them through, but they're of an infinitely higher quality. Then I just don't see the value of the tripwire. It seems like it adds a lot of heat, but no light. There's not a lot of benefit for me, from my perspective, and for seeing it in action. So it doesn't mean don't do it. There's some context where it just makes sense. If you're doing some kind of, I don't know, there's some places where it makes sense, I guess. The fact that I'm not thinking of any. But just also to add something to understand, we've never used a tripwire. I don't know that Andre's ever used a tripwire long before I showed up. I cannot see any scenario in the future where we will be using a tripwire. I can't imagine that scenario. So that's a long answer to a short question. Anything you want to throw in there before we jump to the next one? Yeah, I think the anything I'll add is the type of marketing that we do and we do, and then as a by-product of that we also teach is it's heavily front-loaded with world-building, where we demonstrate value upfront before asking for anything, any transaction. And as Sean said, it's a different type of person that makes their way through that and is attracted and pulled through that and then they raise their hand and they want to opt in. You don't need to put a tripwire in that scenario. In fact, it's, again, I'm just making an assumption here, but I think it would not improve the system. What we've shown inside of this workshop is a funnel. He asked, what else can we build a funnel? How else can we build a funnel? Well, we've just shown you how to build a funnel that can work all day long. It'll generate high-quality leads and if you have something to sell those leads, you can sell stuff to the leads. That's the funnel. And then once that thing's working, you just expand your world and expand it, but that is a funnel for lack of a better term. And Robin just posted something in chat, which by coincidence and very luckily for me, gave me a perfect example of where tripwires can work. So this is Robin's comment in chat. Awesome, Sean. I saw a book on Facebook and I saw a book about how to use a flat-hand saw. I saw boatloads of ten-pound e-books on Facebook. Time to change my angle. I wouldn't for Robin because Robin is an expert in his field and this is where things get a little, this is where I think we can get some gray area. So I saw Robin in his business that an e-book, it's actually a great positioning tool for him. So I can see a scenario where if someone buys into the value proposition that is embodied in the purchase of his book and engages with his book or not, just has him in mind as an authority in that field because he is. It contributes to that sort of a symbol of expertise. That to me makes sense. He's not selling the book to set something else up to sell. He's selling the book so that the lead gets more, he's educating them about what's possible. So I think Robin is a really good example of where you can pull this off and pay for some of your traffic, maybe pay for all of your traffic. It's just what happens after that. It's recognizing that any time we introduce something, we've changed the context for the prospect or the customer. So when we bring somebody through from a long-form Facebook ad to a landing page to opt in for what's next, that's a certain context. When we've brought somebody into a world where we have positioned a book as being a resource in a particular topic, and someone says, I want to buy that, now we've changed their world a little bit. Now that we've given them something to engage with, so after that I would have a three or four part email series that points out things in the book in order to draw their attention to. Because now you know what's happened. They now own the book. The context has changed. They now also see Robin as an authority. So the context has changed. And now Robin is acting on that authority and drawing their attention back to the value they can get in the book in ways that are very helpful. He's guiding them through it. That changes things dramatically. So thank you, Robin, for giving me a perfect example. I never would have come up with on my own. So I want to move on to the next question here. Francis just asked a quick question. I'll just answer this very quickly. Did Ryan Deis build his business on tripwires? I don't know the answer to this question. I know my guess is digital marketer. He's been building his business and learning what he's learning for decades. And his business is a result of decades of work. So it's not really helpful just to look at digital market and think, well, is it built on tripwires? I think there's some of it that has been, but certainly not the whole thing. It's evolved. Yeah, it's a lot easier to build your business on an idea before you tell the entire world about the idea. That's always the danger. If you figure out something that works, it's sort of the Dan Kennedy model. You figure out something that works and then you sell it. This was his model years ago. You figure out something works enough to explain it, then you sell it to everybody else. But then when everybody else knows the thing that you did that works, probably doesn't really work anymore. I think when you're interacting with somebody that's brain used, never seen you, cold leads, I think good images or really hand drawn sketches is a nice way to draw attention and to be different. So it draws attention and gets somebody looking, seeing, I don't know. But yeah, I'm a fan of them. Yeah, I think the only thing I would add is images on landing pages. Absolutely. Because you're controlling that environment images and emails. Just be aware that if you're a Mac user, by default images are displayed in mail. If you're a Windows user using like outlook and other tools like that by default images are turned off in emails. So the way to, I think the solution to this is to, if you use an image always include a caption that sells the image so that if somebody is, if someone's on outlook in a Windows environment and looks down and there's a little red X instead of the image, there's a caption and text underneath it. That says what it is so that they're inclined to enable images so they can see it. So that's a good workaround for email. Just don't never, never assume an email that people see that they're seeing exactly how you've imagined it. Whereas on the landing page, you can. You can rest most likely people are seeing it the way you've laid it out. So yeah, and images certainly if you can pull off good images, they make sense. But don't use like stock. Like be careful. But I found much more value on behalf of my clients if they were getting, they were having real synchronous conversations with other people, and the signals that we would look for were, you know, certainly leads in how many, you know, interest initially booked calls. That was a big one but the really big one was show up rate, like what percentage of people who booked a call actually showed up that told us a lot about the likelihood that they would buy in the future and then, you know, that was just getting them on the call and really in having often the business owner or some proxy who really understood it never a third party, like research or data gather excited to get it because you have to really, you have to, you have to be paying attention to the conversations and and be good at asking follow up questions and really get out, have the ability to sense is this person does this person seem like a prospect who would would buy does this person seem like they're mildly interested. I feel that most of the client I feel like most and actually this is more for me. Alright, I'll answer it and I'll end this one off to you. I feel that most of the clients don't get to play the long game and optimize for happy customers game. They're looking for short term results Sean with your vast experience of doing client work how did you work through this. I quit to an agency work fun. That's the honest answer. This is the all joking aside this is the one of a few critical problems of being a service provider. They're three and this is one of the big three which is that clients, even if if they nod up and down and say I get it I understand why we're playing the long game, I absolutely get they they're actions overwhelmingly don't follow that they are they are looking for short term results. And this can manifest in lots of ways where you know that they get questions about you know what was our cost per lead last week and is it going up or is it going down and just the questions that are, they're just mind numbing so there you have a few options one. Find better customers right that's the heart that's hard and I'm not saying that in a way that's good at all I mean that you. You do have to be picky and and interview your questions it's a good reframe. I did this for a long time. It's a wonderful reframe where. So here's a conventional frame for a service provider. You're pursuing the client, you want to work for the client I would reframe that where the client. It's there. And I didn't ever say it this way with the client is lucky to work with me. And the reality is if you're a service provider, you're being hired you know, by definition you know more than the client does. So, yes, you are the one who should be pursued. If you're treated like a commodity by the client it's a bad client. Period. There's no way around it there's no fix for that. So, I would recommend. This is a this is a long way to answer this question but I think this is the, the most value I can give you Prado to answer this question is to buy David Baker's book the business of expertise. He the book in and of itself is masterful. If you run any kind of creative agency or your service provider is it sort of in this marketing world. That book is the Bible the Quran the fill in the blanks Torah fill in the blanks. It's, it's so good. I just, I cannot even approach explaining the answer this question as well as David Baker has explained it. So start with that book and recognize that, and he has this model of people have to, you don't provide services unless they've done the, the strategy consulting work first and the client is agreeing to implement your strategy. Even on the micro level as an individual service provider you can pull that off. Often you have to educate your clients. And you have to be willing to draw a line in the sand and in not violate your own rules I've violated mine more times than I can count so I'm not saying this from, you know, a whole year than now perspective it's just once you make the decision that you are the expert, then you need to embody expertise, which is you're not putting up with bullshit with people who don't get it, you're going to give them an opportunity to understand how to win at this game that they're trying to play. You're explained that they're making it clear that you're the expert in that game. And if they want to win at they need to follow your strategy and if they want to optimize for the short term they need to go find somebody else who's going to do that because you are not willing to. You can the way I would explain to clients is that I take fiduciary responsibility for their for the relationship meaning I am I look at their what with their spending on ad spend in particular, as I am a steward of that money, and I cannot spend that money in a way that is counter to what they told they've told me their best are. And once I framed it that way, it made the conversations much easier. So when a client would say, Well, you know, we want you to do xyz because the cost per lead is too high, I would remind them you have not hired me to optimize for cost per lead you have hired me to get you happy customers. And because of that, that's how I'm making my decisions. Period. And say, Well, we want to do x that point. It's very clear, we can, we can, I'm not willing to do it the way that you've described because that will not achieve the goal that you have described to me. Period. It's hard to do this but when clients get it, they eventually leave you alone, because they know it's just not worth having the conversation because they start to buy in but this is hard. This is incredibly, incredibly hard. Anything you want to throw on that question, Andre, I'm going to just move on to the next one. That's, that's a great, awesome book. And if you enjoy the book, get on to the podcast to Bob's is brilliant. And they, you know, Bob and his, and his partner crown, not his business partner crown but his podcast partner crown. Um, so for me, the question is more, um, have I, have I, have I, you know, it's really, have I ever worked with clients or done things in ways to borrow Andre's term number the client where I felt like the work the client was doing or that I was being asked to do was shady in some way. Um, I'm not, I'm not thinking of, there's no example that immediately jumps out to me. So I want to say, I don't think so. But at the same time, that doesn't seem plausible to me. So I'm, I'm busily trying to scan through like, can I remember where I was working with a client where I felt like what the client was providing. I really can't. Um, so I, I, and I'm not, I don't want to be very clear. I'm not saying this to suggest in any way, shape or form. Um, that I'm the model of, of professional integrity or it's not, that's not what I'm saying at all. I think for me, there's this phrase that I've probably repeated a million times on various calls and for those of you who've heard me say it before, I apologize. But there's a phrase I, I, it's from Graham Duncan in an interview with, with Tim Ferriss where he talks, he said that our genius is right next to our dysfunction. Um, so I say with, I say genius for myself with humility, but part of my genius is I can kind of see the structure of things and how things flow from A to Z. Like if someone explains something to me and I have enough time to learn to understand it, or if I read a piece of writing or I'm creating something, I see, I kind of see how it flows. And if there's something that interrupts that flow, it, that's where my dysfunction shows up is that I get, I get hung up on it. I can't, I can't, I can't, I can't make sense of it. It doesn't flow for me. It doesn't make sense. So I have worked with clients where I felt like what they were doing and saying didn't flow. Like they, like something that was being said earlier, maybe promises that were being made. I didn't see how they resolved later. And because of that, that incongruence, it bothers me. And it bothers me in a way where I, it can be very paralyzing. And I say this not to, to represent integrity, but to represent that this can be very dysfunctional. That I can't, I have a lot, there's, I have a lot of difficulty moving forward and making progress if, if I sense that the, that the structure of what I'm doing, meaning the, the how things are happening doesn't really make sense. So the best example I can share, that's sort of an easy example is I've been a martial artist for, since I was 12 years old, I got my black belt in Korea when I was 16. I continue to train and if I'm in an environment where I'm training and it feels like the instructor is just kind of doing a flavor of the day, like let's throw a bunch of things out and have people go through a bunch of different things with no thought to it. I really struggle in that kind of environment because I want to know, I tend to think to myself the same things I've shared in some of my writing like first of all, what are we optimizing for here? What's the point? What are we trying to accomplish? And is what we're doing moving us in that direction? And if those things don't line up for me, and if what I'm doing doesn't contribute to what I'm, for the reason why I'm doing it, it's very paralyzing for me in a very weird way. So to bring this back to Cohen's question, which I hope I pronounced your name correctly, to bring it back to that question, then I would have a, I would, it would be very difficult for me to work with a client where I felt like what we were saying anywhere in the journey didn't match up with a reality. So that's a very long explanation, very personal explanation. It's a very long explanation where I don't think so, but I also, I am absolutely human. I know behavior follows incentives and I would be surprised if there haven't been times where an incentive changed my behavior in ways that if I looked back on it now, I would say, and I wish I could kick that guy in the ass and smarten him up a little bit. There's a great question here. I read this earlier. For the book funnel you were talking about do book funnels kind of work because they cover for an end cost so you can keep just keep getting more customers and tiny percent will buy high ticket. That's the first question and then how come you all don't charge more for your stuff in the click funnels world everyone is at 997 plus but your stuff is way more valuable. Thanks Francis. I'll tackle the book funnel part is I just done a million of them and then Andre I'll hand off the pricing question to you because we've talked about that a lot. Okay, book funnel. If you look at a really good book funnel, Mike Gillette is a guy to go look at his book funnel. He's a I don't know his entire backstory but it's g l e t t if you go to Mike Gillette's book funnel. And if you if you look at the book funnel and then think to yourself, oh my God, this guy is doing so much work to sell it a 995 book. That's when you realize that the book funnels not selling the 99, the 995 book book funnel is selling the second upsell. That's that's that's the first change of my put it just saw your chat. I'll French put an E at the end of Gillette. So when you're creating a book funnel, a really good book funnel sells the upsells. That's how it that that's really, you know, Todd Brown talks about this a lot. And Todd is, Todd has the Todd has described this in a way that's real, really genius. The first time I heard it, I disagreed with him. And then, as I thought about it, it's really brilliant. And the central idea is this, when you have a book funnel, any front end funnel, there are really two numbers that you care about cost per acquisition, how much does it cost you to get a customer and average order value. How much do they buy on average, when they become a customer. Todd's insight is most things that reduce CPA are difficult, not reliable, and really hard to move the needle on that like all the conversion rate optimization stuff, it's really hard. However, increasing average order value is infinitely easier than decreasing cost per acquisition. So what that leads to people internalize that idea is that you create a quote unquote book funnel, but there's so much put into that book funnel that you're not actually selling the book, you're selling the second upsell the $250 upsell to drive up your average order value, so that you're at least at break even which is getting customers for free, who you are then trying to sell the core offer to, or you might even be making money on the front end. So that's the short answer to the book funnel question. Second question over to Andre, how can we don't charge more for our stuff. Most people 9997 plus $2,000 is the is the sort of the current conventional price for price range, primarily because half of that needs to go to the affiliate. So a couple of between the thousand and $2,000. And we sell, you know, for half that. So why do we do that Andre. It may come as a surprise that we don't actually look at what other markets do. We do what feels right for us. And one of the things that, you know, everybody knows that we optimize for which is happy customers and in our minds, charging $500 for all of our courses just seems like it's ridiculous value. We want we want customers to be surprised and delighted when they make a purchase at everything they're getting. So that's the balance. It's, you know, we don't want to sell our stuff for $100 because that'll be done. But 500 seems like it's just ridiculous value. And as part of our, our world view of optimizing for happy customers, many, many, many of our customers, a large percentage of them, a decent percentage of them purchase all of our courses. It's because, you know, one of the reasons is because it's such ridiculous value for $500. Well, they want all of them at that point. That would be different if each course costs $2,000. I mean, we could sell them for $2,000. But in again, in our world, that will be done because we don't think that is the is the best path for us to generate as many happy customers as possible. So 500 is just that number that showed up that just felt right. And we stuck with it. So every single course that gets created, when it's finally released, it'll be $500. Well, $495. That's that's the price. If it's unreleased and it's beta, then we, we do even things that even more stupid and we sell them for $200 and something or $300 and something. And again, that's, that's just because that's how we want to show up and and do our stuff works for us. I don't think I really have much to add to that. I mean, it's just, it's a It just comes out. This is, I think the thing that people forget all the time. I teach two entrepreneurship class entrepreneurship classes at Vermont Tech. And one of the things I mentioned to students a lot is that when you become an entrepreneur, you not only are you not only playing a games if you're mad at chess, you're not, you're not playing a game based on somebody else's rules, what you realize as an entrepreneur is you get to make the rules. And there's, there's no rule that says we need to charge. There's, there's, there's just, there's, there's no rule we get to decide like that's part of the freedom of being entrepreneurs and you know that the price point where Andre landed before I showed up and when we've talked about this makes perfect sense to me is that the person who pays for 95 ish running around there. That person is making a significant commitment and we recognize that would we would it change the nature of things if we doubled that commitment, probably not for that person. We would, what would probably happen on balance is that fewer people would get access to what we're creating. And that doesn't feel right. At the same time, $200 wouldn't, wouldn't have the same poll for that person. They wouldn't feel like they've, they've invested enough that it's worth drawing them into it. For some reason that 495 price point in that area, it feels like that it that it's in the person who is buying its best interest. And that's really what we're optimizing for. Yes, we want to make enough money that we can put roofs over our head and pay for some of our bad habits and things like that. But that's not what motivates us. And if, if we're really going to be true to what we teach, which is critically important for both of us that we are that what you see is what you get that you're never going to find out when you pull a curtain back that we've been making up some things along the way. There's to ensure there's never a chance of that happening. Then everything has to have that same level of integrity and the pricing. It seems to be in that neighborhood where it can be, we realize it can be a stretch. But when somebody makes that decision and makes that stretch, we want them to feel like almost like stunned, like, like they really got so much value from it and that that's something money can't buy from our perspective. So that's a, that's all add to that. I think. All right, let's go to, I keep thinking we're getting close, but we're not. So, I'll answer this question that Cohen asked, I really hope I'm pronouncing your name correctly. So Sean, can you add, can you tell more about getting that silver thread out of the work we did, making a Facebook ad to offer emails and how to tie that to our ideal customer and their problems and needs. I think I mentioned that in the recording for in the week five for the promotional emails. That is just for background purposes. I have stolen that idea from animal trackers there, like some of the particularly native or aboriginal very sophisticated animal trackers often talk about when they're, they're following an animal they're seeing a silver line, like, like they're, I don't know if, and I don't know if they mean they're imagining it, but that between them and the animal that they're tracking, there is, there's a silver line that connects them and I don't know, I don't know anything about the nuances of that at all but for some reason that that idea has resonated with me and when I think about writing or crafting a message like from Facebook ad to landing page through seven different emails. I'm imagining that there is this line that connects everything from beginning to end. So in week, I believe is week zero or week, I guess week one with the thematic arc. You can think of it as that line. So that there's, but it's not that it's only for me. Like if I were to, if I were to take, like if somebody handed me your writing, and said here's all my stuff and this is really what we talked about by a lot in the bonus chapter that we released this morning, but if someone were to hand me all of their stuff. I would want to know structurally, I would want to be able to draw that line from like dot to dot to dot through their work to say like, Oh, here's the thread that connects everything I know I'm leading them to this destination on the treasure map and there's an X on it. I know they're way over here on the beach they just landed on the island. And what I'm, my writing is doing is I'm taking them across that trail. Those are my ideas or that trail that leads them to the X on the, on the treasure map. I wish I could explain it better. I can see it in my mind. I can sort of sense it, but it's that how does each thing that you're saying there's a frame that you've set. And then there's this world that you've created. That's kind of the treasure map. That's the island. And then your each idea that you're expressing should lead from one to the next to the next to the next so that when they, when they arrive at the X on the map, your offer. It just feels like that of course that's where they should they arrive like everything you've said has been leading there all along. In the moment it's revealed it's almost like this exhale like, of course, this is what it's been all about. And I think that's, that's the feeling I'm going for. Okay, now I'm not a pronouncing name. So sorry. I don't know if you want to share any thoughts on that. That was my metaphor. I don't know, Andre, you may think about that entirely differently. No metaphor that you use. I love that. Yeah, the only thing I'll add is that, you know, we spend, Sean and I spend a lot of time thinking out loud to each other and brainstorming coming up with with themes for the various things we we're going to create. And it's always more rich and more valuable when we're doing it together, as opposed to when I used to do this myself, or if there's not a collaboration partner to bounce ideas from and it's just in my head. Those ideas are never as good. I mean, you can, you can still come up with with the same or similar themes. Sure. It's just the process is very different and it feels more emergent when it's when there's two people or three people, I suppose, but I suppose at some point, then it gets too many. So I think of anything, fight a writing partner or collaboration partner or something or just customers or a group of people that you hang out with and then just share your idea and have a brainstorm and and that backwards and forwards is going to create emergent ideas that then will inform themes and other, you know, angles that you can use for your for your, you know, for the work you're doing. And I think, you know, conversation we had yesterday is a great example of this. We don't want to give away too much top secret. It's open loop, open loop, open loop, but it's what we were we were working on the structure of something very important. That's coming out soon. It's going to be amazing. But it was two hours. I mean, really two hours of what if we had stopped anywhere between one hour and an hour and 45 minutes, and anybody saw what we accomplished, they would think that we were just monkeys hammering away on a keyboard. And it was, we were it was just we were floundering. But what what was happening is it was it was leading to a place where all of the pieces like a Rubik's cube were turning and turning and turning and all of a sudden, the last one clicked and was like, there it is. And from that emerged the structure, like that made sense and was congruent and could frame everything we're doing on an enormous project. It just, it's so it's that it's something about that like don't give up on it early. If you're frustrated when you're, you're trying to find it that's that's that's the name of the game. It happens, but at some point it kind of clicks. And you could actually do this and you could. I know like john McPhee, the nonfiction writer. He talks about in his book, draft number four, but when he, he writes, or he types his ideas, which are probably what we would call story beats. But he types those and then he cuts the paper up into little slips of paper. So it's like one idea per slip of paper and then he gets an enormous like he gets a four by eight sheet of plywood in his office. And the book, he arranges the book with those little slips of paper and like makes a book outline, it's not the writing, it's the ideas. Like, so if you have it sounds crazy to print out all of your work in a digital world and set it up, but it would be interesting. If you really want to pressure test your work, print out everything that you've written, cut them into ideas and then see to the ideas flow. Do they make sense? Do you say the same thing 12 different times? A lot of the editing we did when you're looking at the work people submitted for this workshop. One of the things I noticed that was most common is people would say the same thing four different ways in three different locations. And it was like, just say it once, right? That's an idea. It's a discrete thing. That's like it's a, it's a point on this line that you're creating. Anyway, all right, let's get off that tangent. Okay, next question. This is from Robin. At what, at what kind of product price point does your world building framework start? It seems more geared toward hyper price products. Andre, your thoughts on that? Maybe I'm not understanding the question, but our world building isn't tied to a price point. It's the narratives and the stuff we put out there. So for us, our world building can start on external web websites in on podcast interviews, but really it starts on our website. So we've got hundreds and hundreds of thousands of words of, of front end website content where somebody can read with that opting in without having any transaction with us other than them interacting with our content. So that's our, that's our, our framing or our world view for what world building is and, and where it starts. And then it leads to an email opt in or a purchase. And then, then the world building continues an email in the courses that we create. It's, that's all part of the world building. So I think that's, in fact, the, the video that's inside of the bonus lesson for that we shared today, the one with our Martin, I think that's his name. But, you know, that whole video is, most of the video is on, on how he's talking about world building. And that's really, I've just, we've just taken that metaphor and applied it to, to the stuff that we create, which is our version of world building. And I think that, yes, there's products that are part of that, that, that world. But it doesn't have to start like that. I think that's, that's, that's my answer. That's my final answer. And I like, you know, Seth Godin is, I think in an interview at one point said that a brand is a promise. Different, different brands make different promises. I think he uses the example of like the Hilton hotel versus like a cheap hotel. It's a different promise. And when we build worlds, I think we're, we're making a promise. And that, and it's, it's a context specific promise, but it's this idea that when you're interested in X, Robin is the person who like, that's the, that's the person that's that's who we want somebody to think of. The world that you're building and the framing for that world is when somebody is interested in the topic where you are the expert, that they look to you first, and they trust over time, their, their barriers to trusting you are dropping significantly because you've demonstrated time and time again that you're the expert and that you have their best interest at heart. So when we're, we think about world building, it's not necessarily a time bound event as much as it is a way of showing up in the world where you are the logical conclusion to a question that's asked about your expertise. So if, for me, if I decided to get back on my bicycle at some point, and wanted to go fast, I, I would reach out to Jonathan Baker because I know in my world, the person who is the brand promise to me of Jonathan Baker is he is the person who knows how to train people to go faster on a bicycle that's his promise. The world in which he inhabits is that world. And that's, I think that's what a lot of world building is about is that we're, it's a way to contextualize and make sense of another person and say, when you have this set of challenges or you want this type of outcome, or you want to learn more about this subject. This is the person who you go to ideally first, or this is one of several people you go to. I think that that's how I would describe it. All right. Next question, Brian, any advice on how to best target audiences on Facebook using the momentum to build a workshop should we make our Facebook targeting more broad. Short advice best advice I can give you on Facebook is make a large audience with a clear optimization event and let Facebook Facebook algorithm work on your behalf. So Facebook's algorithm 400,000 data points. If you if in the challenge of course is what do you optimize for in the short term I would optimize for leads in the longer term. I mean if you have enough volume I would optimize for purchases. The challenge with optimizing for purchases is, you know, as as of Monday we think this the iOS 14 updates going to drop on Monday which is going to make it that's going to sort of have wreak havoc on a bunch of Facebook tracking issues, and it's also Facebook is reducing its conversion window down to seven days so it's unlikely in the scenarios that we're describing, you're going to get somebody to become a customer in seven days, and the momentum to build a workshop structure so knowing that I would optimize for leads. The problem with leads it's not that's a precursor to what you actually want to have happen so I wouldn't get too caught up in that but broad audience, optimize for leads, but don't try to optimize for leads in your way of writing, let Facebook optimize for leads to find your audience within the big audience, but don't do anything to kind of goose that or your, you know, you're offering a lead magnet or some sort of incentive don't do any of that. And I think that's the sort of middle ground combination. All right, next question. I'm a writer with a full time job with an agency I write quite a lot for my clients which leaves me little energy and also little to write copy for myself. I hope you would agree that writing is hard work. Any suggestions on managing this energy. A few suggestions. Andre, any suggestions on energy management. Yeah, I don't have a good answer for this. Other than perhaps, you know, treat yourself as a, as a client. So you become a client for yourself. And if there's no slots and if you decided that you can only do 10 client slots, I'm just using that as an as an idea. And those 10 clients lots are filled up. Well, you don't have any more time to do anything else. So when there's, when there's nine slots or eight slots, well, now you can consider yourself as you can select yourself in there now and then schedule out the time as if you were doing it for a client and do it for yourself. I don't do client work. So it's difficult for me to, to answer that in any other way. Yeah, so the way I would think about this, and I've actually dealt with this in the past two. So I have some thoughts on it. First thing I would do is figure out when you have peak energy in general, just at, and that's unique to you. And broadly speaking, it's probably in the morning or it's probably late at night. Those seem to be the two, the two extremes. For me, for example, I'm a far better writer in the morning, although I get a weird second wind late to occasionally. But the reason you need to do that is, once you know when you are at your creative best, then you want to set aside that period of time, even if it's only 30 minutes of that time, whatever it is, you want to set aside that time to write every single day and into rate consistently. Don't worry about how much you write each day, just write every day. And it's consistency over intensity. And what you'll find is that the compounding interest of time passing produces results far beyond what you would expect. You know, sit down one day, you'll just feel like you didn't accomplish anything. It's just, you know, day to day, you don't feel like you accomplished anything and you look back. And over the course of a month, you know, you wrote thousands and thousands of words. So consistency over intensity, aligned when you write with your peak creative time. All of this, though, is within the context of what are you optimizing for. Right. So if you're optimizing for being a copywriter who produces great work for the agency, then then that's a very different story. Then you need to align your peak creative time with the most demanding clients that you have or the clients that produce the most reward, however, you're measuring that reward. But if you're optimizing for creating something for yourself, that changes things that and you have to respect that. And the last thing I'll mention here is everything that you want. So, you know, from an energy management perspective, you know, and there's certainly there, there are ways you can hack it, you know, caffeine, great ally nicotine, the great ally nuts don't smoke it but other ways to consume you can boost your energy. That's, I don't really recommend that. I mean, lion's mane is good for like clarity of mind. There are lots of ways you can kind of you can hack it. But where the power where your power exists is in making a decision. And years ago, I someone explained this, I assume this is true, maybe it's not true, but the Latin root of the word decision is means to cut. So a decision is to cut off all alternatives. And the moment you make a decision that you're going to write every day. And you cut off all alternatives come hell or high water, you write every day. And the moment you do that, everything changes. You don't have to, you know, all this bullshit about it 60 days to create a habit that hell with habits and give a shit about habits. I care about decisions. If I decide that I'm going to write every day, I'm gonna write every day. I don't need to wait until my nervous system catches up and says hey it's a habit I can, I've made in broken habits so many times in my life that it's, it's ridiculous. It's ridiculous. So, but decisions, all of our power exists within a decision. And the moment you make the decision that you're a writer and you're going to write 30 minutes every day and there and you're no exceptions 100% much easier than 99% no exceptions. That's where all of your power then you don't need to worry about energy. You don't need to worry do I feel like writing that with that, right. If every day at 9am or whatever let's say you get up early at 6am, between 6am and 630am every single day you write. And there's no it you there's never accept an exception you're a writer at that point you're doing the work. Period, you don't need to manage anything you don't need to think about anything, but your ass in the chair, fingers on the keyboard, you're a writer period. All right. I found myself realizing that I was writing too early about my framework and hence I repeated a lot in the landing page and emails. When is a good moment in the ad the landing page of the emails one through three to introduce my framework and when to share the details and what to implement. That's such a this is a great question. So hint at your framework early, open the loop to your framework as early as the ad, and you can do that in a way or you know if you've introduced the problem or the desire. I would I would actually make this a thread, this sort of silver thread that would be that knowing the frameworks coming. I would hint at it a lot I'd hit it in the ad I didn't add it landing page, and I would certainly hint at it in the emails and hinting at it can be mentioning that there is a framework. So they're looking forward to it. Mentioning a part of it, like what's what's one thing or what's one element of it, and then saying but later I'll put that in the context of the entire framework. Now I'm curious. What is this framework, they just create lots of opportunities to reference this thing that's coming that's really valuable, so that when like you're almost getting there and backing away almost getting there and backing away giving a little bit, but not all of it. And it's not. Then you were when you finally reveal all of it. Just keep in mind that you are you are now resolving a lot of tension. So in the reveal, as you resolve that tension. You also have to create more tension for what you're actually going to sell them later, which are the results that this framework creates. So as you would. So if you can imagine it, you're, you're hinting at your so you're creating a little tension you're hinting at and not completely revealing what the framework is and then you're describing but not completely resolving that tension. And at some point, you're, you're doing the masterful thing that happens in good writing, which is you are resolving the tension by describing this framework you've been hinting at all the time, but actually increasing the tent the overall tension because that framework isn't the whole story. It's part of the actual story that you're revealing to them through the purchase of your product. So that's that that's how that's how I would think about how and when to reveal that hint at it. Explore it a little show like just you're showing your they can peek at it peek at it you've revealed it but the big reveal is that it's not the framework. It's how their life changes on the other side of having that framework, which is what you're going to explain to them next now you've just jacked up the tension again, and you continue to pull them forward. Right that in that no one wants to hear from us unless we've it's all we've dotted all the the eyes and crossed all the teas and this is our final thinking. And it takes to do that so that it doesn't just seem like it's just hey they have some ideas and then they write a course and sunshine and unicorns life's not like that and we don't ever want anybody to think it's that easy because we're not in the lying to you that it's easy business. We don't sell easy we sell reality and we don't sell this hey we tried this thing and it worked once so therefore anybody can do it what we what we are selling is if we look at across what has been successful over four decades.
